what_it_does: Design-only document that specifies how to make the cc_channels router transport-aware by adding a supervised transport for headless session-supervisor clones, so they can register, be pushed messages, and reply in the same group threads as interactive channel sessions.
observations: Defines two transports: channel (interactive MCP server on a port) and supervised (headless session owned by a supervisor).
Registration records in .data/channels/ gain a transport field; portful legacy records default to channel for back-compat.
Proposes moving cc_clone clone registrations from .data/clones/ into .data/channels/ with transport:supervised.
live_sessions() prunes channel records by pid/port and supervised records by supervisor /sessions status.
push() dispatches to HTTP POST for channel or supervisor /inject for supervised.
Replies from both transports fold into the same mail log and thread index via route_reply.
send() and broadcast() keep the same API; mixed channel/supervised groups open one thread.
Explicitly removes the operator gate for autonomous clone-in-group while keeping it for interactive channel clones.
Build split assigns cc_clone registry changes to the fork and cc_channels dispatch/reply changes to the lead.
Requires surfacing the change to Tim before landing because it alters core router semantics and autonomy boundaries.
Acceptance tests extend tests/cc_channels_acceptance.py with back-compat, dispatch, liveness, reply fold, and mixed-group cases.
Risks include supervised reply correlation, watcher lifecycle, and shared-registry ownership.
standouts: Introduces a supervised transport that lets headless clones join group chats autonomously without an interactive channel port.
Generalizes the existing channel router to be transport-aware while leaving the send/broadcast API unchanged.
Explicitly frames the change as an autonomy-increase decision that must be surfaced to Tim.
concerns: Design changes core router semantics but is still pre-implementation, so behavior details remain speculative.
Supervised reply correlation depends on turn order and per-member serialization; concurrent injects are not modeled.
Watcher daemon lifecycle for option B is only sketched, not fully specified.
Unifying cc_clone and cc_channels registries into one directory creates cross-owner coupling and cutover risk.
The autonomy increase is flagged but not accompanied by concrete mitigations beyond existing supervisor controls.
notes: Pre-implementation design contract that makes cc_channels transport-aware so supervised headless clones can join group chats alongside interactive channel sessions.
questions: What is the exact shape of the supervisor /watch and /inject endpoints?
How is the last_thread map stored and updated for supervised sessions under option B?
How does cc_clone atomically write a supervised registration into .data/channels/?
What happens when a broadcast targets a supervised member that is already busy mid-turn?
How is the per-supervised-session watcher daemon started, stopped, and reaped?
Will .data/clones/ be retired or kept as a private mirror, and how is consistency maintained?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches the content: it is a design for cross-session unified transport that lets cloned sessions join group chats autonomously.
apparent_themes: Differentiated autonomy/safety boundaries for interactive vs supervised sessions
Single durable mail log and thread index across transport modes
Explicit ownership and review gates before core semantic changes
intention_signals: Extend the channel router so agent-spawned, supervisor-controlled headless clones are first-class conversation members, enabling time-travelled past-context clones to participate in group threads while keeping unsupervised interactive launches operator-gated.
novelty: Proposes treating a session-supervisor inject/watch path as a peer transport alongside HTTP channel push, folding it into the existing mail/thread model without changing the public send/broadcast API.
contribution: Contributes a concrete transport-aware routing design and a registry-unification contract that lets autonomous supervised clones participate in group threads with the same mail-log semantics as interactive channel sessions.
summary_for_embedding: Design document for adding a supervised transport mode to cc_channels so headless session-supervisor clones can register, receive pushes, and reply in the same group threads as interactive channel sessions.
intention_for_embedding: Intended to let cloned-at-a-point-in-time sessions join group chats autonomously via the supervisor while keeping interactive channel launches operator-gated.
